retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: HER2-positive breast cancer: Trastuzumab is FDA-approved in combination with anthracycline or taxane-based chemotherapy. The maximum recommended duration of adjuvant treatment with trastuzumab is 12 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Genes and Broad-Spectrum Drug Approvals
retrieved_docs.content: The landscape of tumor-agnostic therapies continues to evolve, with trastuzumab deruxtecan making strides toward tissue-agnostic approval. On April 5, 2024, the FDA approved this drug, marking the first tumor-agnostic indication for an antibody-drug conjugate and the first broad-spectrum approval for a HER2-targeted therapy. This approval applies to patients with unresectable or metastatic HER2-positive tumors who have an IHC 3+ status.

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Evaluation for HER2 Tumor Marker -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The American Society of Clinical Oncology/College of American Pathologists (ASCO/CAP) guidelines for HER2 testing reporting and critical findings were updated in 2023. The update reaffirms the 2018 "HER2 Breast Testing Guideline Focused Update" and was prompted by the results of the 2022 DESTINY-Breast 04 trial, which led to the expansion of the approval of the HER2 antibody-drug conjugate, trastuzumab deruxtecan, by the United States Food and Drug Administration (US FDA) to include metastatic patients with HER2 immunohistochemistry (IHC) 1+ or 2+/in situ hybridization (ISH) negative results. However, the guideline update does not support the use of a HER2-Low interpretive category due to the lack of evidence to support that IHC 1+ or 2+/ISH negative results are predictive of trastuzumab deruxtecan treatment response when compared to IHC 0 results. [21] [22] The guidelines recommend the following best practices for HER2 testing:

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: HER2-positive gastric cancer: Trastuzumab is administered with cisplatin-based chemotherapy, a combination that modestly improves survival (median 13.8 months vs. 11.1 months). [2] [3] Trastuzumab deruxtecan is a newer antibody-drug conjugate associated with positive outcomes, particularly during later-line therapy. As a first-line treatment, the combination of pembrolizumab, trastuzumab, and chemotherapy demonstrated a remarkable response rate, prompting its quick FDA approval. [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer: Trastuzumab is approved as monotherapy or in combination with paclitaxel. [6] In June 2020, the FDA approved a combination of pertuzumab, trastuzumab, and hyaluronidase (subcutaneous) for patients with HER2-positive early-stage and metastatic breast cancer. [7] Trastuzumab has also been administered in combination with lapatinib, anastrozole, cyclophosphamide, pertuzumab, and several other agents to treat metastatic breast cancer. [8] [9] [10] [11] According to the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO), HER2-targeted therapy is recommended for patients with HER2-positive advanced breast cancer, except for patients with congestive heart failure or a significantly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, which should be evaluated individually. As a first-line treatment, ASCO recommends trastuzumab, pertuzumab, and taxanes. If pertuzumab is not available, clinicians may offer a combination of chemotherapy and trastuzumab. [6] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Colorectal cancer: The FDA approved trastuzumab and tucatinib as treatments for colorectal cancer in January 2023. [12] This combination is approved specifically for HER2-positive, chemotherapy-refractory, RAS wild-type unresectable, or metastatic colorectal cancer. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: HER2-positive (IHC 3+) unresectable or metastatic solid tumors: Trastuzumab deruxtecan is administered to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic HER2-positive (IHC 3+/immunohistochemistry 3+) solid tumors who have received prior treatment and have no sufficient alternative treatment options. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Trastuzumab is a biologic antineoplastic agent that was FDA-approved in 1998, making it one of the first available “targeted” chemotherapies. Trastuzumab deruxtecan is a trastuzumab conjugate that releases its cytotoxic components after binding and entering HER2-expressing cells, effectively treating HER2-positive gastric, lung, colorectal, and metastatic breast cancer. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non-small cell lung cancer: Trastuzumab deruxtecan is approved for HER2-mutant (unresectable or metastatic) NSCLC in adults who have previously received systemic therapy. [4] Per the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) 2024 guidelines regarding Stage IV Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer with driver alterations, clinicians may offer treatment with trastuzumab deruxtecan. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Trastuzumab is an FDA-approved antineoplastic biologic that targets HER2-positive breast and gastric cancer. This activity outlines trastuzumab's various indications and off-label uses, including its combination with anthracycline or taxane-based chemotherapy for breast cancer, as monotherapy or in combination with paclitaxel for metastatic breast cancer, and in combination with cisplatin-based chemotherapy for gastric cancer. This discussion also includes the mechanism of action, administration methods, significant adverse effects, FDA-issued box warnings, toxicity profile, and monitoring requirements of trastuzumab. Understanding these elements is crucial for tailoring therapy to meet patient needs, optimizing dosing regimens, and minimizing adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Trastuzumab is a biologic agent primarily used to treat HER2-positive breast cancer and may be used as adjuvant therapy for localized disease or as first-line treatment for metastatic disease. The availability of trastuzumab has significantly increased survival associated with HER2-positive breast cancer; notably, trastuzumab also carries considerable historical importance as one of the first “targeted” chemotherapeutic agents developed. Trastuzumab is typically given in combination with one or more cytotoxic agents, most commonly anthracyclines (eg, doxorubicin) and taxanes (eg, paclitaxel). Trastuzumab has also demonstrated efficacy in the treatment of HER2-positive metastatic gastric cancer. The most significant adverse event associated with trastuzumab is cardiotoxicity; this is usually mild and reversible with discontinuation, but it may progress to irreversible, clinically significant cardiac failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Evaluation for HER2 Tumor Marker -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Several HER2-targeted therapies have US FDA approval for breast cancer, including trastuzumab, pertuzumab, ado-trastuzumab emtansine, lapatinib, trastuzumab-deruxtecan, and tucatinib. Additional therapies are still under clinical trials. The presently used agents include:

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: In November 2020, the FDA's Peripheral and Central Nervous System Drugs Advisory Committee met to discuss the aducanumab BLA. A series of 4 votes culminated in the near-unanimous (there was a single abstention) verdict that the advisors did not find reasonable grounds to consider the results of the clinical trials as evidence of aducanumab's effectiveness for treating AD. Within days of the July 2021 accelerated approval decision, 3 members of the Advisory Committee, comprising one-third of its nongovernment members, resigned, citing "serious potential to impair future research" and deeming the FDA's ruling "the worst approval decision that FDA has made....." [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab is a humanized IgG1 monoclonal anti-programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1) antibody that has been approved by the U.S. Food Drug Administration (FDA) for various neoplastic conditions as a single agent or in combination with other chemotherapeutic agents. These FDA-approved indications are discussed below. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Afatinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Afatinib is a medication that targets and irreversibly inhibits the ErbB family of tyrosine kinases. [1] This drug is FDA-approved for managing locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) associated with nonresistant epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) mutations. Additionally, afatinib is a second-line treatment of advanced squamous non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). [1] Afatinib is also being investigated as monotherapy in patients with HER2-positive breast cancer who had progressed despite trastuzumab treatment. However, it does not yet have FDA approval for this indication. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: to canertinib dihydrochloride, an epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitor, which is also related to the monoclonal antibody margetuximab, highlighting occasional overlaps between local anesthetic taxonomy and targeted cancer therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, mechlorethamine, vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlormethin hydrochloride, liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, and pembrolizumab are all agents that may be used to treat مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, mechlorethamine, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide), a microtubule inhibitor (vincristine sulfate), a DNA‑intercalating anthracycline formulation (liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride), a bleomycin family antitumor antibiotic (bleomycin sulfate), a targeted antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), a miscellaneous cytotoxic compound (chlormethin hydrochloride), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab). Their combined inclusion reflects the multimodal approach often employed in managing Hodgkin disease, where chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy may be selected based on disease stage, patient characteristics, and prior treatment response.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1445
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic Substance is a broad category that encompasses a diverse group of therapeutic agents, each identified as a value for a specific GDC property used in genomic and clinical data integration. Among these agents are ethinylestradiol, a synthetic estrogen commonly employed in hormonal therapies; barbituráty, which denotes the class of barbiturates and indicates that certain pharmacologic substances belong to this subclass; brentuximab vedotin, an antibody‑drug conjugate indicated for CD30‑positive lymphomas; chlorambucil, an alkylating chemotherapy drug; chlormethin hydrochloride, a chemotherapeutic agent; doxorubicin hydrochloride, an anthracycline antibiotic used in a wide range of cancers; hydrochlorid prokarbazinu (procarbazine hydrochloride), an alkylating agent used in combination regimens; ifosfamid (ifosfamide), a nitrogen‑mustard alkylating agent; lomoten (lomustine), a nitrosourea chemotherapy drug; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor employed in immuno‑oncology; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor used to stimulate white blood cell production. By being designated as values for the GDC property, each of these pharmacologic substances is recognized within a standardized framework that facilitates consistent annotation, data sharing, and clinical decision‑making across research and patient‑care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1391
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic substances represent a broad category of therapeutic agents that are identified as values for a GDC property, encompassing a diverse array of drugs such as ethinylestradiol, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, doxorubicin hydrochloride, hydrochlorid prokarbazinu, ifosfamid, mechlorethamin, oxidopamin, pembrolizumab, vincristine sulfate, and vinorelbin tartarát. Within this category, barbituráty (barbiturates) are recognized as a specific subclass, illustrating that barbiturates are themselves pharmacologic substances. The inclusion of these agents under the same pharmacologic substance classification highlights their shared role in medical treatment despite differing mechanisms of action: ethinylestradiol is used in hormonal therapy, cyclophosphamide anhydrous and ifosfamid function as alkylating chemotherapeutic agents, doxorubicin hydrochloride is an anthracycline chemotherapy drug, pembrolizumab serves as an immune checkpoint inhibitor, vincristine sulfate and vinorelbin tartarát act as microtubule‑targeting anticancer agents, and compounds such as mechlorethamin, oxidopamin, and hydrochlorid prokarbazinu have specialized pharmacologic applications. Recognizing these drugs as pharmacologic substances under a common property facilitates integrated understanding of their therapeutic uses and supports coordinated patient care across multiple treatment modalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic Substance is the overarching category that includes a diverse set of agents such as ethinylestradiol, barbituráty, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlorambucil, chlormethin hydrochlorid, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, hydrochlorid prokarbazinu, ifosfamid, pembrolizumab and streptozocin. Barbituráty are identified as a subclass of pharmacologic substances. Each of the listed compounds—ranging from the estrogenic hormone ethinylestradiol, the anticonvulsant class barbituráty, the chemotherapeutic agents bleomycin sulfate, chlorambucil, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, ifosfamid, streptozocin and prokarbazinu, the targeted antibody‑drug conjugate brentuximab vedotin, the immunotherapy checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab, to the antihistamine chlormethin hydrochlorid—are recognized as values for a GDC (Genomic Data Commons) property, indicating their relevance in genomic and clinical data repositories. This classification underscores the importance of these substances across multiple therapeutic areas, including oncology, hormonal therapy, immunotherapy, and neurological disorders, and facilitates their integration into precision‑medicine frameworks and patient‑care decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic Substance is a broad category that includes a diverse array of agents such as ethinylestradiol, barbiturates, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlormethin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, lomustine, pembrolizumab, sargramostim, and vinblastine sulfate. These compounds represent multiple therapeutic classes: ethinylestradiol is a synthetic estrogen used in hormonal therapy; barbiturates are central‑nervous‑system depressants; bleomycin sulfate, ifosfamide, vinblastine sulfate, and lomustine are cytotoxic chemotherapeutic agents; brentuximab vedotin and pembrolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target specific cancer antigens and immune checkpoints; sargramostim is a recombinant cytokine that stimulates granulocyte‑macrophage colony formation; and chlormethin hydrochloride and procarbazine hydrochloride are alkylating agents used in oncology. All of these substances are recorded as values for a specific data‑catalog property associated with the Pharmacologic Substance class, highlighting their shared classification while underscoring their distinct mechanisms of action and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1919
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic substances represent a broad class of therapeutic agents that includes a diverse array of specific compounds. Ethinylestradiol is a pharmacologic substance used primarily as an estrogen component in hormonal contraceptives. Barbituráty are classified as a type of pharmacologic substance, reflecting their role as central nervous system depressants. Bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlorambucil, dakarbazin, and doxorubicin hydrochloride are pharmacologic substances employed as chemotherapeutic agents in the treatment of various cancers. Pembrolizumab is a pharmacologic substance that functions as an immune‑checkpoint inhibitor, enhancing antitumor immune responses. Prokarbazin, sargramostim, and vinorelbin tartarát (vinorelbine tartrate) are also pharmacologic substances, with sargramostim acting as a cytokine growth factor and vinorelbin tartarát used as a chemotherapy drug. Together, these examples illustrate the extensive range of pharmacologic substances that clinicians utilize across multiple therapeutic areas, highlighting their importance in patient care and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1290
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and gilteritinib are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group designated “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” indicating that each drug belongs to this specific category of protein kinase–targeting agents. This classification reflects their shared mechanism of action as protein kinase inhibitors within the broader ATC system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1261
retrieved_docs.content: Vintafolide is classified as a targeted therapy agent.
